data.title: Tuesday’s Economic Calendar
data.description:
FOMC Meeting Begins 

The Federal Open Market Committee meets eight times a year to determine the near-term direction of monetary policy. 

9:00 AM Case-Shiller Home Price Index

The S&P CoreLogic Case-Shiller home price index tracks monthly changes in the value of residential real estate in 20 metropolitan regions across the nation. 

9:00 AM FHFA House Price Index

The Federal Housing Finance Agency (FHFA) House Price Index (HPI) covers single-family housing, using data provided by Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac.

10:00 AM Consumer Confidence

The Conference Board's confidence report surveys consumers on their assessments of the labor market, business activity, and their own financial conditions.

Confidence is expected to take a hit from worries about rising prices and uncertainty flowing from the Iran war. The consensus sees the index down at 89.4 in April from 91.8 in March.

10:00 AM Richmond Fed Manufacturing Index

This survey tracks business conditions in the Richmond Fed's manufacturing sector.

11:30 AM 2-Yr FRN Note Auction 

An FRN (floating rate note) is a security that has an interest payment that can change over time.

1:00 PM 7-Yr Note Auction

Treasury notes are sold at regularly scheduled public auctions. The competitive bids at these auctions determine the interest rate paid on each Treasury note issue.

1:00 PM Money Supply

The monetary aggregates are alternative measures of the money supply by degree of liquidity.

data.title: Simpson outlines 19.5%-20.5% 2026 operating margin as U.S. housing starts expected down low single digits
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Simpson Manufacturing Co., Inc. (SSD) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We delivered net sales of $588 million, up 9.1% from the prior year quarter" (CEO, President & Director Michael Olosky), adding that growth was "primarily driven by our 2025 pricing actions" plus "foreign exchange," while "partially offset by an approximate 1% decline in volume as a result of softer housing start activity."
  • "The component manufacturer business delivered a strong quarter with volumes up double digits, driven primarily by new customer wins" (CEO Olosky), and management tied demand to "productivity-enhancing solutions" across "software, plates, equipment and design services" as truss manufacturers prioritize "labor efficiency, throughput and operational visibility."
  • "Our consolidated gross margin declined 130 basis points year-over-year to 45.2%" (CEO Olosky), attributing pressure to "higher material, factory and tooling and labor cost" and "start-up costs from the ongoing ramp of our Gallatin facility," while saying "our 2025 price increases, which we now expect will contribute approximately $130 million in annualized net sales helped offset these costs, including those attributable to tariffs."
  • "Pricing actions are contributing as expected and are projected to add roughly $130 million in annualized net sales, helping offset some tariff-related cost pressures" (CFO & Treasurer Matt Dunn), while also emphasizing, "we do not expect this level of revenue growth to carry through the remainder of the year."

Outlook

  • "We now believe 2026 housing starts in the United States will be down low single digits compared to 2025" (CEO Olosky), versus the prior quarter’s stance that housing starts would be "relatively flat year-over-year" (CEO Olosky, Q4 call).
  • "We continue to expect our consolidated operating margin to be in the range of 19.5% to 20.5%" (CFO Dunn), reiterating the same full-year range given last quarter (CFO Dunn, Q4 call).
  • "Additional key assumptions include... a lower overall gross margin based on imposed tariffs and increased depreciation costs" and "an expected $3 million to $5 million of footprint optimization costs in Europe" (CFO Dunn), plus "a projected $10 million to $12 million benefit on the sale of vacant land in the back half of 2026."
  • "We haven't taken any additional price increases in North America beyond the 2 that we announced last year" (CFO Dunn), while noting management may reassess if costs change: "potentially have to look at that if things change. But right now, nothing in the works."

Financial Results

  • "Net income totaled $88.2 million or $2.13 per fully diluted share" on "net sales" of "$588 million" (CFO Dunn).
  • "Consolidated gross profit increased 6.1% to $265.9 million, resulting in a gross margin of 45.2%" (CFO Dunn), and he quantified a key swing factor: "start-up costs in our Gallatin facility represented an approximate 100 basis point headwind to our first quarter gross margin."
  • "SG&A headcount was down approximately 9.1% year-over-year" and "operating expenses were 25.6%" of net sales (CFO Dunn), with "onetime costs in Q1 2026 of $2.3 million related to our strategic cost savings initiatives."
  • "We repurchased $50 million of our common stock" and ended the quarter with "a net debt position of $29.5 million" (CFO Dunn), while saying the company remains "focused on disciplined capital deployment" and "our commitment to return at least 35% of free cash flow to shareholders."

Q&A

  • Dan Moore, CJS Securities, Inc.: "talk about the... cadence of demand and volumes in North America" after the "start of the war in Iran and spike in oil prices"; CEO Olosky: "it's been a bit of a soft selling season," and he said the company triangulated forecasts from "6, 7 different firms" plus customer checks.
  • Dan Moore, CJS Securities, Inc.: "Have you taken or contemplated any additional price increases"; CFO Dunn: "we haven't taken any additional price increases in North America" and management is "really focused on maintaining and kind of preserving our gross margins."
  • Dan Moore, CJS Securities, Inc.: land-sale timing; CFO Dunn: "It's definitely going to be in the back half... TBD, whether it's Q3 or Q4."
  • Trey Grooms, Stephens Inc., Research Division: end-market assumptions; CEO Olosky: repair and renovation "flattish to maybe up 1-ish percent," commercial "low single-digit range," and OEM "low single digits."
  • Kurt Yinger, D.A. Davidson & Co., Research Division: $130 million vs. $100 million pricing; CFO Dunn: "We're upping the annualized number to $130 million" from prior guidance and tied the change to "surcharges" and "price increases" in Europe plus North America mix toward "fasteners and anchors."
  • Timothy Wojs, Robert W. Baird & Co.: size of component manufacturing; CEO Olosky: "We have not commented on the size of the component manufacturing business."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative to neutral, with questions probing macro risk and cost pass-through, including "spike in oil prices" (Dan Moore, CJS Securities, Inc.) and whether Europe could see "further risk" (W. Andrew Carter, Stifel).
  • Management: slightly positive in prepared remarks but more cautious on forward demand, including "we do not anticipate sustaining the same level of revenue growth" (CEO Olosky) and "we do not expect this level of revenue growth to carry through" (CFO Dunn).
  • Versus last quarter: management language moved from "relatively flat" U.S. housing starts (CEO Olosky, Q4 call) to "down low single digits" (CEO Olosky), while keeping the operating margin range unchanged.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • "We now believe 2026 housing starts... will be down low single digits" (CEO Olosky) compared with the prior quarter’s "relatively flat" expectation (CEO Olosky, Q4 call).
  • "We now expect [2025 price increases] will contribute approximately $130 million in annualized net sales" (CEO Olosky) versus the prior quarter expectation of "at least $100 million" (CEO Olosky, Q4 call).
  • Analysts shifted toward near-term demand sensitivity and cost recovery mechanics (fuel, freight, tariffs, and Europe surcharges), while management repeatedly emphasized margin preservation and cost actions.

Risks and Concerns

  • "Near-term market conditions remain difficult" and the company "do[es] not anticipate sustaining the same level of revenue growth" (CEO Olosky), with housing starts expected to be "down low single digits" (CEO Olosky).
  • "Input cost headwinds" in Europe required "surcharges and price increases" (CEO Olosky), while in North America management highlighted monitoring costs like "fuel" and "potentially steel" without new price actions (CFO Dunn).
  • Margin headwinds included tariffs and ramp costs: "start-up costs in our Gallatin facility" (CFO Dunn) and "imposed tariffs" in the full-year framework (CFO Dunn).

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 around price realization and cost actions offsetting tariffs and ramp costs, while emphasizing mix-driven growth areas such as component manufacturing software and OEM/mass timber. At the same time, leadership tempered expectations for the rest of 2026, updating U.S. housing starts to down low single digits and cautioning that Q1’s revenue growth rate is not expected to persist, while maintaining the full-year operating margin outlook of 19.5% to 20.5% and pointing to a back-half land-sale gain and ongoing capital returns, including buybacks under the $150 million authorization.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Cadence forecasts 17% 2026 revenue growth, outlines $6.125B-$6.225B outlook as Hexagon integration weighs on margins
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Cadence Design Systems (CDNS) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Cadence had a strong start to 2026 with accelerating AI demand and disciplined execution, delivering one of the best Q1s in company's history" and "Our record backlog of $8 billion was ahead of plan" (CEO, President & Director Anirudh Devgan).
  • "Given the accelerating momentum of our business, we are raising our 2026 revenue growth outlook to 17% and expect to achieve the Rule of 60 for the first time" (CEO Devgan).
  • "We announced a strategic collaboration with Google to optimize the ChipStack AI Super Agent with Gemini on Google Cloud" and "we announced an expanded partnership on AI and robotics with NVIDIA" (CEO Devgan).
  • "Our IP business continued its strong momentum, with 22% year-over-year revenue growth" and "Demand for our hardware accelerated in Q1, resulting in our best quarter ever" (CEO Devgan).
  • "Total revenue was $1.474 billion" and "GAAP EPS was $1.23, and non-GAAP EPS was $1.96" (Senior VP & CFO John Wall).

Outlook

  • "For our updated outlook for 2026, we expect revenue in the range of $6.125 billion to $6.225 billion" and "non-GAAP operating margin in the range of 43.5% to 44.5%" (CFO Wall).
  • "For Q2, we expect revenue in the range of $1.555 billion to $1.595 billion" and "non-GAAP EPS in the range of $2.02 to $2.08" (CFO Wall).
  • "The updated outlook" included "the Hexagon design and engineering business" and management said this was a change versus the prior-quarter outlook, which "does not include our pending acquisition of Hexagon's design and engineering business" (CFO Wall; CFO Wall in Q4 call).

Financial Results

  • "Robust design activity, coupled with our solid execution, drove 19% year-over-year revenue growth and 45% operating margin for Q1" (CFO Wall).
  • "Operating cash flow was $356 million" and "we used $200 million to repurchase Cadence shares" (CFO Wall).
  • "First quarter bookings were ahead of expectations" and "DSOs were 67 days" (CFO Wall).

Q&A

  • Yu Shi, Needham & Company: concerns about AI writing software and whether it threatens base-tool defensibility; CEO Devgan responded, "I'm very confident in our position in the base tool and our competitive advantage" and "I'm not worried that some other party will be able to write any better base tools."
  • Jason Celino, KeyBanc: asked why operating margin guide moved down; CFO Wall said it was "primarily the impact of including the Hexagon design and engineering business" and added, "We expect it to be dilutive to the tune of about $0.28" and "We'd expect it to be accretive in 2027."
  • Vivek Arya, BofA: asked how shortages and customer pricing power affect engagement; CEO Devgan said, "the environment is pretty healthy" and noted some customers may use "multiple foundries or nodes" which "would directly lead to more design activity for us."
  • James Schneider, Goldman Sachs: asked how agentic tools are priced and how Cadence captures value; CEO Devgan said new automation would be "priced as a subscription plus consumption model" and described agents running "10 or 100 variations" that drive more base-tool usage; CFO Wall added, "the overall pricing environment has improved" and "Pricing obviously remains value-based."
  • Sitikantha Panigrahi, Mizuho: asked on IP drivers and foundry contribution, and about EPS guide bridge; CFO Wall answered, "we did... raised by $0.08," while CEO Devgan highlighted portfolio expansion and said on Intel Foundry, "we are making very good progress with Intel, and we'll have -- soon, we'll have more to say."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was slightly negative to probing on defensibility, margins, and guide prudence, including questions about "defensibility" (Needham) and margin guide "coming down" (KeyBanc).
  • Management sentiment was positive in prepared remarks and confident in Q&A, including "I'm very confident" (CEO Devgan) and "the overall pricing environment has improved" (CFO Wall).
  • Compared with the prior quarter, management kept a similarly confident tone on AI and workload expansion, while the current call added more integration-related explanations and near-term dilution detail tied to Hexagon.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Backlog increased to "$8 billion" in Q1 2026 from "$7.8 billion" exiting 2025 (CEO Devgan; CEO Devgan in Q4 call).
  • Guidance framing shifted: Q4 guidance explicitly said it "does not include" the Hexagon transaction, while Q1 guidance incorporated it and CFO Wall described 2026 as "an integration year" with near-term dilution.
  • AI narrative broadened from launching ChipStack in Q4 ("the world's first Agentic AI solution") to a broader "agentic full-flow platform" in Q1 with "AgentStack," "ViraStack," and "InnoStack" (CEO Devgan; CEO Devgan in Q4 call).

Risks and Concerns

  • "Our updated outlook... contains the usual assumption that export control regulations that exist today remain substantially similar for the remainder of the year" (CFO Wall).
  • On Hexagon integration, CFO Wall said, "2026 is an integration year" and cited acquisition dilution drivers including "lost interest income on the cash" and "acquisition-related integration costs," with mitigation implied in, "We'd expect it to be accretive in 2027."

Final Takeaway

Cadence management characterized Q1 as a "strong start" with "record backlog of $8 billion" and raised its 2026 revenue outlook to "approximately 17% year-over-year growth" while introducing a fuller agentic AI platform strategy and multiple partnership expansions. Management also emphasized that the updated 2026 outlook now includes Hexagon’s D&E business, which CFO John Wall said would be "dilutive to the tune of about $0.28" in 2026, with accretion expected in 2027.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: California fuel imports surge following refinery closures - Argus
data.description:
California's gasoline and jet fuel prices have jumped to multiyear highs, even as imports have soared, Argus reported Monday, as the state has become increasingly dependent on fuel imports following the closure of two large refineries.

California gasoline imports surged to 142K bbl/day in March, nearly triple the amount from a year earlier, after spiking to 122K bbl/day in February from 28K bbl/day, as Valero's 145K bbl/day Benicia refinery started to shut down, according to Argus, which cited data from Kpler.

Tightening regional supply and higher underlying Nymex RBOB values due to the Middle East war pushed prices higher, including Los Angeles regular CARBOB differentials that rose to a five-month high of $0.60/USG on April 21, more than $0.41/USG above pre-war levels, Argus said.

At the same time, in-state CARBOB production fell in mid-April, heightening supply constraints, as California refiners cut production of CARBOB gasoline 13% to 570K bbl/day, which Argus said was the lowest in at least five years.

Meanwhile, jet fuel prices on the west coast reached an all-time high of $4.92/USG on April 23, up from a previous high of $4.81/USG on March 20, according to Argus data; California jet fuel stocks had tumbled to a more than two-year low as of April 10.

South Korea—California's top supplier of jet fuel—relies heavily on the Middle East for its crude and naphtha supplies; Californian jet fuel arrivals from South Korea have averaged ~38K bbl/day so far in April, roughly even with March but down from 46K bbl/day in January-February, Argus reported.

ETFs: (USO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UGA)

data.title: Amkor expects Q2 EPS of $0.42-$0.52 as Arizona ramp is set to dilute operating margin by 1%-2% starting in 2027
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Amkor Technology (AMKR) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Amkor delivered a strong start to the year, achieving record first quarter revenue of $1.68 billion, up 27% year-on-year," said CEO Kevin Engel, adding that "earnings per diluted share were $0.33" and the quarter reflected "continued preparation for the advanced packaging ramps we expect in the second half of the year" (CEO, President & Director Kevin Engel).
  • Engel flagged supply-chain and macro variables, saying, "We are closely monitoring export controls and evaluating trade policies" and "some customer supply materials are being delayed, causing nonlinear loading"; he also said, "conditions in the region are putting additional pressure on material pricing" while noting, "to date, we have not seen any supply disruptions" related to Middle East dynamics (CEO Engel).
  • On advanced packaging and capacity, Engel said, "we are engaged on several HDFO programs this year and the newest data center CPU program is expected to begin ramping this quarter," and he framed site expansion priorities as "meeting construction milestones of our Arizona facility and expanding manufacturing space in Korea," with "construction of Phase 1... planned to be completed in 2027" and a Korea test building "on track for completion at the end of this year" (CEO Engel).
  • CFO Megan Faust highlighted end-market drivers and profitability, saying, "gross margin of 14.2% exceeded the high end of our Q1 guidance range primarily due to favorable product mix" and "as we have grown revenue by delivering high-value advanced packaging technology to our customers, we are benefiting from the operating leverage in our model" (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Megan Faust).

Outlook

  • Faust guided Q2 revenue to "between $1.75 billion and $1.85 billion" with gross margin "between 14.5% and 15.5%" and EPS "between $0.42 and $0.52" (CFO Faust).
  • Faust said Q2 operating expenses are expected to be "approximately $120 million which includes a gain on the sale of real estate of approximately $20 million," while "our 2026 CapEx estimate remains at $2.5 billion to $3 billion" (CFO Faust).
  • On Arizona start-up timing and margin impact, Faust said, "we anticipate this will start to dilute operating income margin by approximately 1% to 2%, beginning in 2027 and improving in 2028," and added, "once at full scale, we expect Arizona will be a significant driver of operating income margin expansion" (CFO Faust).
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s call, management reiterated it was "confident in the full year outlook we provided last quarter," while Engel updated communications to "higher into low double digits" versus prior "single digits" language and cautioned, "we're a little bit hesitant to say that the first half, second half dynamic will be the same for this year" (CEO Engel).

Financial Results

  • Faust reported Q1 gross profit of "$239 million," operating income of "$100 million" and net income of "$83 million"; she also reported "EBITDA was $285 million and EBITDA margin was 16.9%" (CFO Faust).
  • Balance-sheet metrics were described as "$1.8 billion in cash and short-term investments" and "total liquidity was $2.9 billion" with "total debt... $1.4 billion" and "our debt-to-EBITDA ratio was 1.1x" (CFO Faust).
  • End-market commentary included that communications was supported by "healthy demand across premium tier smartphones, especially iOS" and that computing saw "record revenue within AI data center applications" but "softness in PCs and laptops" (CFO Faust).

Q&A

  • James Schneider, Goldman Sachs: asked about "what on-net you expect to happen in terms of gross margins in the back half" amid material delays and pricing pressure; CFO Faust answered, "we expect that would cover most of those cost increases" and said gross margins could "rise in that mid- to high teens level" supported by "pricing, utilization and product mix" (Executive VP Faust).
  • James Schneider, Goldman Sachs: asked whether the computing ramp inflects in Q3 or is "more... Q4 weighted"; CEO Engel answered the CPU device "will start this quarter" with "meaningful revenue contribution in the third quarter" (CEO Engel).
  • Benjamin Reitzes, Melius: asked about the timing of the "1- to 2-point hit" in 2027 from Arizona; CFO Faust said "it's a bit too early" and framed it as "a full year impact" estimate, adding Arizona revenue would be "some modest revenue in 2028" and "meaningful revenue" exiting 2029 (Executive VP Faust).
  • Randy Abrams, UBS: asked about utilization and headroom; CEO Engel said Q1 utilization was "in the low 70s" and described Vietnam SiP migration as a way to "provide additional room in Korea" while noting "we have continued room in Vietnam to grow" (CEO Engel).
  • Peter Peng, JPMorgan: asked whether AI advanced packaging growth is demand- or supply-constrained; CEO Engel said, "we're still on track for tripling" while noting supply of "silicon" and "memory" could affect outcomes (CEO Engel).
  • Denis Pyatchanin, Needham: asked about material supply impact; CEO Engel said, "I would try to give that a range of around $50 million to $100 million" and expected "a similar level in Q2" (CEO Engel).
  • Joseph Moore, Morgan Stanley: asked about export controls; CEO Engel said it was partly "trade discussions... between the U.S. and China" and added, "it's not a dynamic that has a huge impact on what we're looking at today" (CEO Engel).

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to neutral, probing margin durability, Arizona dilution timing, utilization headroom, and supply constraints, including questions about "puts and takes" on margins and whether AI packaging growth is supply-limited (Goldman Sachs, Melius, UBS, JPMorgan).
  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more measured in Q&A, emphasizing pricing actions and offsets ("constructive pricing environment") while acknowledging uncertainty ("we're a little bit hesitant") and deflecting detail on EMIB ("I don't want to talk too much about that") (Executive VP Faust; CEO Engel).
  • Versus last quarter, the call preserved confidence language around 2026 growth drivers, but incorporated more explicit discussion of material delays, nonlinear loading, and pricing pressure tied to geopolitics (CEO Engel).

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Management’s strategic pillars were consistent, but Q1 added more specific operational milestones: Engel said the "newest data center CPU program" begins ramping "this quarter" and highlighted construction progress in Arizona ("wrap up foundation work") and Korea test expansion (CEO Engel).
  • Guidance framing shifted from Q4’s seasonally-low Q1 setup to Q2 acceleration, while also introducing a clearer multi-year margin bridge for Arizona, including the "1% to 2%" operating margin dilution starting in 2027 (CFO Faust).
  • Analyst focus broadened from last quarter’s heavy CapEx/funding emphasis to include second-half margin trajectory, utilization, and how supply constraints may alter the ramp shape (Goldman Sachs, UBS, JPMorgan).

Risks and Concerns

  • Engel cited risk areas including "export controls," "trade policies," and supply tightness in "advanced silicon, advanced substrates and memory," plus "nonlinear loading" from delayed customer materials (CEO Engel).
  • Engel said Amkor is "prioritizing production where materials are available" and working with customers on pricing, while Faust said pricing efforts should "cover most of those cost increases" (CEO Engel; Executive VP Faust).
  • Faust flagged execution and timing risk around Arizona qualification, stating 2027 timing "can shift based on the timing of equipment delivery as well as the speed of qualification process" (Executive VP Faust).

Final Takeaway

Amkor’s management positioned Q1 as a record start to 2026 and guided to higher Q2 revenue, margin, and EPS on stronger-than-expected demand outside computing PCs, rising utilization, and an advanced packaging mix shift led by a data center CPU HDFO ramp beginning in Q2 with meaningful contribution targeted for Q3. Executives emphasized offsetting material-cost pressure through pricing and described supply delays as driving nonlinear loading rather than broad utilization disruption, while also laying out a more explicit Arizona investment and margin cadence that includes a 1%-2% operating margin dilution starting in 2027 before scaling benefits later in the decade.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Boise Cascade pleads guilty, fined $6.38M by DoJ in timber trafficking scheme
data.description:
Boise Cascade (BCC) down 2.2% post-market Monday after pleading guilty and sentenced to pay a $6.38M fine for violating federal law for its role in a timber trafficking scheme, the U.S. Department of Justice said.

The DoJ said the company purchased, sold, and transported hardwood plywood from Horizon Plywood that it knew, or should have known, was illegally imported from China to evade countervailing and anti-dumping duties.

The importation was illegal because Horizon smuggled the wood and violated the Lacey Act by falsifying import declarations for hardwood and softwood plywood, the DoJ said.

The fine represented twice the gross profits it derived from the illegal wood at issue in this case, and the company also was sentenced to implement a compliance plan.

data.title: Sanmina forecasts FY2026 revenue $13.7B-$14.3B while targeting $16B+ in FY2027
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Sanmina Corporation (SANM) Q2 fiscal 2026

Management View

  • "We delivered strong results for the second quarter. Revenue came in at $4.01 billion and strong non-GAAP operating margin of 6.4% and non-GAAP diluted EPS of $3.16. Cash flow from operations was also strong at $399 million." (Co-Founder, Executive Chairman & CEO Jure Sola)
  • "Our revenue of $4.0 billion came in well above our outlook range... customer demand for the ZT systems business resulting in some accelerated compute shipments previously expected in the second half to shift into the second quarter." (Executive VP & CFO Jonathan Faust)
  • "Today, we announced that our Board of Directors authorized an additional $600 million of share repurchases." (Executive VP & CFO Faust)
  • "Book-to-bill was better than 1.1." (Executive Chairman & CEO Sola)

Outlook

  • "We expect revenue between $3.2 billion and $3.5 billion" for Q3, alongside "non-GAAP operating margin of 6.4% to 6.9%" and "non-GAAP diluted earnings per share in the range of $2.55 to $2.85." (Executive VP & CFO Faust)
  • "We expect revenue to be in the range of $13.7 billion to $14.3 billion" for FY2026, with "non-GAAP operating margin... between 6.3% to 6.6%" and "non-GAAP diluted earnings per share... $10.75 to $11.35." (Executive VP & CFO Faust)
  • "ZT Systems revenue is down compared to the prior quarter due to the accelerated compute orders that shifted from the second half into the second quarter." (Executive VP & CFO Faust)
  • "Based on what is in front of us, we are increasingly confident in our ability to achieve revenue of $16 billion plus in 2027." (Executive VP & CFO Faust)

Financial Results

  • "Revenue came in at $4.01 billion" with "non-GAAP operating margin of 6.4%" and "non-GAAP diluted EPS of $3.16." (Executive Chairman & CEO Sola)
  • "Our non-GAAP gross profit was $360 million or 9.0% of revenue" and "non-GAAP operating profit of $257 million or 6.4% of revenue." (Executive VP & CFO Faust)
  • "ZT Systems business revenue was $1.88 billion, exceeding our expectations." (Executive VP & CFO Faust)
  • "Cash and cash equivalents were $1.58 billion" and "we had no outstanding borrowings on our $1.5 billion revolver." (Executive VP & CFO Faust)
  • "Free cash flow was $342 million" and "we repurchased approximately 1.1 million shares for approximately $160 million." (Executive VP & CFO Faust)

Q&A

  • Ruplu Bhattacharya, BofA Securities: "What drove that outperformance?" on ZT and whether it was "MI300" or "NVIDIA"; Executive Chairman & CEO Sola: "A customer wanted to pull in some of the product" and "We did not ship any accelerated compute of NVIDIA product. Whatever we shipped, it was all based on AMD technology."
  • Ruplu Bhattacharya, BofA Securities: sustainability of ZT margin profile; Executive VP & CFO Faust: "It all depends on the mix of the business" and "long term... we expect the margin profile to be roughly in line with core Sanmina."
  • Ruplu Bhattacharya, BofA Securities: communications demand and advanced products; Executive Chairman & CEO Sola: "Yes, we are shipping 1.6 terabyte switches" and said 5G wireless is "maybe 2%, 3% of it."
  • Samik Chatterjee, JPMorgan: timing and breadth of hyperscaler engagement for next-gen systems; Executive VP & CFO Faust: "Now that's scheduled to be out in September" and "The opportunity in our fiscal 2026 isn't really huge. That's more of an FY '27 play."
  • Steven Fox, Fox Advisors: cash generation with growth; Executive VP & CFO Faust: "almost $400 million in cash flow from operations" and said the company will "start to build inventory levels... to prepare for that new accelerated compute business."
  • Mehdi Hosseini, Susquehanna: diversification beyond AMD and potential R&D needs; Executive Chairman & CEO Sola: "Yes. Yes. Yes, definitely, definitely will" on needing additional R&D as the business shifts toward more value-add.
  • Anja Soderstrom, Sidoti: supply chain disruption risk; Executive Chairman & CEO Sola: "there's some material shortages around the memory, custom ASICs" and Executive VP & CFO Faust: "everything that we're aware of today has been factored into our guide."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts slightly negative to neutral, pressing most on ZT volatility and durability, including pull-ins and implied future quarterly ramps, as reflected in "What is giving you confidence" and detailed timing questions. (Bhattacharya, BofA Securities; Chatterjee, JPMorgan)
  • Management slightly positive in prepared remarks and more guarded in Q&A on timing, repeatedly anchoring to mix and schedules, including "it all depends on the mix of the business" and "it's all about timing" when addressing forward demand. (Executive VP & CFO Faust; Executive Chairman & CEO Sola)
  • Compared with the prior quarter, management language shifted toward higher confidence on FY2027 scale, moving from "we expect fiscal 2026 to be a growth year" to "we are increasingly confident in our ability to achieve revenue of $16 billion plus in 2027." (Executive VP & CFO Faust)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Prepared metrics and cash flow stepped up quarter-to-quarter, with Q2 described as "Revenue came in at $4.01 billion" and "Cash flow from operations... $399 million" versus Q1 "Revenue came in at $3.19 billion" and "cash flow from operation of $179 million." (Executive Chairman & CEO Sola)
  • Guidance tone tightened around a Q3 normalization after the Q2 pull-in, with Q2’s upside attributed to shipments shifting timing, while Q1 guidance had emphasized "tariffs and the geopolitical landscape" as key uncertainties for Q2 forecasting. (Executive VP & CFO Faust)
  • Capital return messaging escalated from Q1 "approximately $160 million remaining on our current share repurchase program" to Q2 adding "an additional $600 million" authorization. (Executive VP & CFO Faust)
  • Analyst focus broadened from parsing ZT transition mechanics in Q1 to deeper platform timing, customer breadth, and margin durability in Q2, while management repeatedly emphasized readiness for next-gen accelerated compute and capacity buildout. (Executive VP & CFO Faust; Executive Chairman & CEO Sola)

Risks and Concerns

  • "There’s some material shortages around the memory, custom ASICs" and management tied this to day-to-day execution, saying "we could have shipped a little bit more if we got able to get every part number that we needed." (Executive Chairman & CEO Sola)
  • ZT revenue volatility risk was acknowledged through timing dynamics, with management noting Q2 reflected a pull-in and that future order patterns can be "nonlinear." (Executive VP & CFO Faust)
  • CPS margin pressure drivers were identified as investment-related, with "depreciation and other expenses related to investments to support new programs" and a product business impacted by "component shortages" that management said it "believe will be resolved in the second half of the fiscal year." (Executive VP & CFO Faust)

Final Takeaway

Sanmina management framed Q2 as a milestone quarter driven by accelerated compute demand and execution, highlighted by $4.01 billion in revenue, 6.4% non-GAAP operating margin, and $399 million in operating cash flow, while emphasizing that the ZT Systems upside partly reflected a customer pull-in of AMD-based shipments. For Q3, management guided to $3.2 billion to $3.5 billion of revenue and $2.55 to $2.85 of non-GAAP diluted EPS, and it reaffirmed FY2026 targets of $13.7 billion to $14.3 billion of revenue and $10.75 to $11.35 of non-GAAP diluted EPS. Management also underscored a larger capital-return posture with a new $600 million repurchase authorization, while flagging ongoing component shortages and timing-driven volatility as key operational constraints heading into the second half and the next-generation platform ramp aimed at "$16 billion plus" revenue in 2027.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Rambus anticipates Q2 non-GAAP EPS of $0.65-$0.73 while projecting $192M-$198M revenue
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Rambus (RMBS) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “We opened 2026 with a strong first quarter, meeting our financial targets and broadening our portfolio to address the accelerating demands of AI,” said CEO Luc Seraphin, adding, “we are well positioned to capitalize on the market trends in the data center and AI.”
  • On chips, CEO Luc Seraphin said, “We delivered product revenue of $88 million in Q1, in line with our guidance and up 15% year-over-year,” and added, “we expect to deliver double-digit product revenue growth in the second quarter.”
  • On the new LPDDR-based server module push, CEO Luc Seraphin said Rambus introduced “our chipset for JEDEC-standard LPDDR5X SOCAMM2 modules,” calling it “the first offering in our road map of LPDDR-based server module solutions,” and said the SOCAMM2 chipset “enables power-efficient, reliable operation of up to 9.6 gigabit per second.”
  • On silicon IP, CEO Luc Seraphin said Rambus saw “growing traction for our value-added PCIe retimer and switch IP,” introduced “the industry's fastest HBM4E controller,” and launched “a new network security engine designed for Ultra Ethernet.”
  • From the CFO, Interim CFO John Allen said, “We delivered first quarter revenue and earnings in line with our guidance,” and added, “We also continued our strong track record of cash generation.”

Outlook

  • Interim CFO John Allen guided, “We expect revenue in the second quarter to be between $192 million and $198 million,” including “product revenue to be between $95 million and $101 million,” “royalty revenue to be between $72 million and $78 million,” and “licensing billings between $76 million and $82 million.”
  • Interim CFO John Allen said, “Overall, we anticipate Q2 non-GAAP earnings per share to range between $0.65 and $0.73,” and guided “Q2 share count to be 110 million diluted shares outstanding.”
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s call, management’s framing shifted from a Q1 headwind to normalization: CEO Luc Seraphin said in Q1 Q&A, “the issue that we have talked about in the prior call is behind us. Everything has been resolved,” while also emphasizing, “it is behind us.”

Financial Results

  • Interim CFO John Allen reported Q1 non-GAAP revenue of $180.2 million, including “Royalty revenue was $69.6 million,” “licensing billings were $70.8 million,” “Product revenue was $88 million,” and “Contract and other revenue was $22.6 million.”
  • On profitability and costs, Interim CFO John Allen said, “Total operating costs, including cost of goods sold for the quarter were $104.6 million,” “Operating expenses of $69.9 million were up sequentially due to seasonal payroll-related taxes in connection with equity vesting,” and “non-GAAP net income for the quarter was $69.3 million.”
  • On cash and balance sheet, Interim CFO John Allen said Rambus ended Q1 with “cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities totaling $786 million,” driven by “strong operating cash of $83 million,” and said, “Free cash flow in the quarter was $66.3 million.”
  • On inventory posture, Interim CFO John Allen said, “We increased our inventory balance by $14 million during the quarter and expect to continue building inventory strategically in the second quarter,” adding it provides flexibility “to support our product revenue growth and manage through potential supply chain constraints.”

Q&A

  • Kevin Garrigan, Jefferies: asked why Q2 product revenue recovery looked “more measured” after the prior quarter’s one-time OSAT issue; CEO Seraphin responded, “the issue…is behind us,” adding, “the revenue for Q2 is guided at 11% over Q1,” and, “We don't see any issue with the demand.”
  • Kevin Garrigan, Jefferies: asked when SOCAMM2 chipset revenue starts; CEO Seraphin answered, “The financial impact in the short run this year is going to be very minimal…So I wouldn't put it in the model for 2026,” while calling it “strategically very, very important.”
  • Tristan Gerra, Baird: pressed on shortages and what changed vs. a quarter ago; CEO Seraphin said, “since last quarter, the situation has not improved,” adding, “the lead times are long, and there is tension on the back end,” and said supply could stay tight “going into 2027.”
  • Tristan Gerra, Baird: sought more MRDIMM ramp detail; CEO Seraphin said, “we continue to see the ramp starting in 2027 in earnest,” and reiterated “a SAM at this point in time, which we still value at about $600 million.”
  • Aaron Rakers, Wells Fargo: asked about MRDIMM attach-rate assumptions and CXL; CEO Seraphin said, “we model a low attach rate,” and on CXL added, “We are not planning to launch a semiconductor product at this point in time,” while noting “very good traction on our IP business.”
  • Gary Mobley, Loop Capital: questioned whether unusually strong licensing/contract trends implied higher silicon IP run-rate; CEO Seraphin said, “we see very good traction on our silicon IP business,” and, “we continue to expect this business to grow 10% to 15% a year,” while adding licensing is “stable at $200 million, $210 million” on average.
  • Sebastien Naji, William Blair: asked for SOCAMM economics and RDIMM companion-chip progress; CEO Seraphin said, “the content on a SOCAMM…is minimal,” while Interim CFO Allen said, “The newer products…are contributing low double-digit percent of our total product revenue during the first quarter.”
  • Mark Lipacis, Evercore ISI: asked causes of backend tightness and market share trajectory; CEO Seraphin attributed tightness to “increased demand…combined with semiconductor companies moving their supply chains outside of China,” and on share said, “we exited '25, we were mid-40% share,” adding, “there's no sign of any erosion of the share.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to pressing on supply tightness and visibility, including “shortages,” “constraint,” and timing dependencies for MRDIMM/Gen 5, with repeated questions on backend capacity and attach rates.
  • Management’s tone was slightly positive and often confidence-leaning on execution while acknowledging constraints, including CEO Seraphin’s “we feel quite confident for the rest of the year,” alongside “the situation has not improved” on supply.
  • Versus the prior quarter, management language moved from explaining a “onetime supply chain issue” affecting Q1 to repeatedly stating it was resolved, while keeping a cautious posture on industry backend constraints.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Finance leadership changed: Q1 featured “John Allen - Interim CFO,” versus Q4’s CFO Desmond Lynch.
  • The operational narrative shifted from Q4’s discussion of quarantining/rescreening and a “low double-digit million” Q1 impact to Q1’s emphasis that the OSAT issue “is behind us,” with guidance centered on sequential product growth.
  • Product commentary moved from Q4’s record quarter and 2025 product growth emphasis to Q1’s DDR5 Gen 2-to-Gen 3 transition focus and building inventory to support ramps.
  • Q1 introduced a clearer expansion theme into LPDDR-based server modules (SOCAMM2) and highlighted specific IP launches (HBM4E controller, Ultra Ethernet security engine) as new portfolio messages.

Risks and Concerns

  • CEO Seraphin said backend supply constraints persist: “the lead times are long, and there is tension on the back end,” and later said tightness is driven by “increased demand…combined with semiconductor companies moving their supply chains outside of China.”
  • Platform timing remained a key dependency: CEO Seraphin said MRDIMM and DDR5 Gen 5 ramps are “completely dependent on the timing of the ramps of the next-generation platforms for Intel and AMD.”
  • On SOCAMM2, management repeatedly framed near-term revenue as limited: CEO Seraphin said 2026 impact is “very minimal” because “the volumes are very small.”

Final Takeaway

Rambus management described Q1 as execution “in line with our guidance,” reiterated that the previously discussed OSAT-related disruption “is behind us,” and guided to higher Q2 revenue and EPS ranges while calling out continuing backend supply tightness and long lead times. Management emphasized DDR5 share position (“mid-40%”) and a Gen 2-to-Gen 3 transition, while positioning SOCAMM2 LPDDR5X chipset and new AI-oriented silicon IP (HBM4E, PCIe retimer/switch, Ultra Ethernet security) as portfolio expansions, with MRDIMM and DDR5 Gen 5 ramps tied to Intel/AMD platform timing and expected to build meaningfully in 2027.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Crude oil gains as U.S. weighs Iran's latest proposal with Strait of Hormuz still shut
data.description:
Crude oil futures closed higher Monday, with Brent crude rising for the sixth straight session, as the oil market doubts the Trump administration will accept a new Iranian proposal to open the Strait of Hormuz on condition the U.S. lift its blockade and postpone negotiations related to Iran's nuclear activities.

A ceasefire has broadly held since early April, but blockades of the strait by Iran and the U.S. have reduced shipping on the waterway to near zero.

"The trade is increasingly skeptical that anything is going to get done anytime soon, and the move to the upside is gaining traction," Mizuho’s Robert Yawger said in a note. "The longer the strait stays closed, the higher the price will rise."

"The diplomatic standoff means that every day 10M-13M barrels of oil fail to get to the international market, worsening an already tight ​oil balance. Therefore, there is only one direction for oil prices to go," PVM Oil Associates analyst Tamas Varga wrote.

Goldman Sachs raised its year-end oil price forecasts, now expecting Brent crude to average $90/bbl and WTI to average $83/bbl, up from previous estimates of $80/bbl and $75/bbl, respectively.

The forecast assumes Persian Gulf exports normalize by the end of June while expecting the market to swing from a surplus of 1.8M bbl/day in 2025 to a deficit of 9.6M bbl/day in Q2 2026.

Front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for June delivery gained 2.1% to $96.37/bbl, its highest settlement value in two weeks and seventh gain in nine sessions, and front-month Brent crude (CO1:COM) for June delivery jumped 2.7% to $108.23/bbl, up for six consecutive sessions and its best settlement value since April 7.

Additionally, front-month Nymex natural gas (NG1:COM) for May delivery rose 1.1% to $2.550/MMBtu.

ETFs: (USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG), (XLE)

data.title: Bed Bath & Beyond targets $60M cost removal over next 9 months as it integrates acquisitions
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Bed Bath & Beyond (BBBY) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Marcus Lemonis said the quarter showed early traction from a multi-quarter restructuring and the “Everything Home” ecosystem, stating, “We delivered revenue of approximately $248 million, up 7% year-over-year or 9.4% when you exclude our discontinuing operations from Canada, which marks the first time in 18 -- 19 quarters that this business has delivered year-over-year growth.”

  • Lemonis tied the quarter to a lower operating baseline and ongoing M&A execution, saying, “Earlier this quarter, we completed the first acquisition of our omnichannel pillar with the Kirkland's transaction,” and adding, “Additionally, we announced the deal to acquire The Container Store.”

  • Lemonis described the strategy as capability-driven, not scale-driven: “We are not acquiring companies for the sake of scale, we are acquiring capabilities,” and added, “We preserve what works, we remove what does not work, and we connect everything through a single system.”

  • CFO Adrianne Lee summarized Q1 performance and liquidity, stating, “Gross margin landed at 23.9% for the quarter,” “All in, adjusted EBITDA came in at a loss of $8 million,” and “We ended the quarter with cash, cash equivalents and restricted cash of $163 million.”

  • President Amy A. Sullivan emphasized store fleet discipline and tech integration, saying, “We’re pairing that strength with a fleet of more than 320 stores,” and adding, “Across the fleet, we are evolving our store formats with clearer roles and stronger economics while taking a disciplined approach to underperforming locations through repositioning, consolidation or exit where returns do not meet our thresholds.”

  • Lemonis disclosed leadership changes, saying, “I want to thank Adrianne Lee for the years of service,” and “Brian LaRose… will be joining our company,” while Sullivan said, “Kyla, who we announced this afternoon is leading our technology transformation.”

Outlook

  • Lemonis reiterated the company’s near-term growth posture, saying, “We are holding steady with the guidepost that we provided earlier in the year, which is low to mid-single-digit revenue growth.”

  • Lemonis flagged integration-related costs that will affect near-term comparisons: “As we get into Q2, we will have onetime operating expenses,” and “I believe that if you looked at what we had laid out in the guidepost, Q2 will have about $13 million of onetime expenses.”

  • Lemonis described the next phase of cost actions and operating model intent, stating, “All can expect that over the next 9 months… we will remove an additional $60 million of cost out of the consolidated company,” and adding, “We are going to become an organization that puts its payroll in the field… and does not put its payroll in corporate offices.”

  • Analysts’ estimates were provided, but the estimates JSON did not use a valid quarter format (Q1–Q4), so no estimates comparison is included.

Financial Results

  • Lee reported operating trends that management linked to mix and efficiency, stating, “AOV improved 6%,” “Orders delivered increased by almost 1% in the period,” and “Sales and marketing expense had improved efficiency of 50 basis points as a percent of revenue versus last year.”

  • Lee highlighted cost reductions in overhead, stating, “G&A and tech expense of $36 million decreased by $5 million year-over-year or $8 million if you exclude the impact of onetime costs from acquisition-related activities.”

  • Lee detailed earnings and cash flow, stating, “Reported adjusted diluted EPS was a loss of $0.25 per share,” and “Cash used in operating activities improved year-over-year by more than $39 million or 77%,” while adding, “In the quarter, we invested approximately $26 million in acquisition-related activities.”

Q&A

  • Steven Forbes, Guggenheim Securities, LLC: asked about how Bed Bath & Beyond merchandise will be introduced into The Container Store and the sales-per-square-foot plan; CEO Marcus Lemonis answered, “Across the 100 locations, there was 2.2 million square feet of retail… half of that… was wildly underutilized,” and added, “It is my goal to take it from an average of about $220 per square foot… to $500 a square foot within 24 months.”

  • Steven Forbes, Guggenheim Securities, LLC: asked whether the planned $60 million cost removal would lead to positive free cash flow; Lemonis said, “It is my belief that… the $60 million of eliminated costs puts us way ahead of needing to worry about being cash flow positive,” and added, “My goal is to get this business to a 6% to 7% EBITDA margin business.”

  • Thomas Forte, Maxim Group LLC: asked how management decides to build, buy, or partner across the three pillars; Lemonis said, “It’s very simple for me,” and described mapping an “11 or 12 years” homeowner lifecycle to identify gaps, while President Amy A. Sullivan said, “We announced our partnership with Bilt this morning,” and described it as a “red thread” tying brands together.

  • Thomas Forte, Maxim Group LLC: asked about converting Container Store locations to AI compute centers; Lemonis answered, “The answer is no,” and added, “We are going to turn our business into an AI-centric business.”

  • Alicia Reese, Wedbush Securities Inc.: asked what other uses the underutilized Container Store footprint could serve; Lemonis said, “We want to make sure that every single square inch… are intensely utilized,” and added, “I would expect that within 24 months… the revenue coming out of those 22,000 square feet… should be double.”

  • Alicia Reese, Wedbush Securities Inc.: asked about Overstock product mix and cross-banner strategy; Sullivan said, “Overstock specifically has really been headed in the right direction,” and Lemonis added marketplace expansion language: “Even by the end of the year… we will be selling cars.”

  • Jonathan Matuszewski, Jefferies LLC: asked how to measure cross-marketing and customer-file integration; Lemonis described creating “one single data layer,” a “single sign-on,” and a second identifier for “the home itself,” adding, “You could expect all of that data to live on blockchain.”

  • Bernard McTernan, Needham & Company, LLC: asked whether Q1 performance changes annual thinking and about macro pressures; Lemonis said, “We haven't seen gas prices change our demand trajectory,” and added that quarterly results will show “core revenue… core margin… core operating expenses,” alongside one-time integration items.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was inquisitive and sometimes skeptical on execution details, with pointed questions on monetizing square footage, cross-selling milestones, and whether cost removals translate into cash generation, including a challenging prompt on “AI compute centers.”

  • Management tone was confident and directive in both prepared remarks and Q&A, using firm language such as CEO Marcus Lemonis’s “The answer is no,” and forward-leaning targets like, “My goal is to get this business to a 6% to 7% EBITDA margin business.”

  • Versus last quarter, management’s language moved from stabilizing and sequencing integration to emphasizing tangible year-over-year revenue growth and specific integration mechanics (store economics, headcount reductions, and stated one-time costs).

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • In Q1 2026, Lemonis emphasized the first year-over-year revenue growth in “18 -- 19 quarters,” while Q4 2025 commentary centered on revenue decline tied to “margin integrity over headline revenue.”

  • Q1 2026 introduced more concrete integration targets and store productivity goals (including $500 sales per square foot in 24 months) and a quantified cost-removal target (“$60 million” over “the next 9 months”), while Q4 2025 focused more on sequencing expectations (“Q2 should be viewed as an integration quarter”).

  • Analyst focus shifted from Q4’s KPI stabilization (active customers, CAC experiments, and early-year growth bridge) to Q1’s specifics on The Container Store footprint, cost-out translation to cash flow, and the pace and measurability of cross-ecosystem synergies.

Risks and Concerns

  • Lemonis warned of near-term integration noise, stating, “As we get into Q2, we will have onetime operating expenses,” and described work including “purging systems and terminating leases.”

  • Lemonis described planned reductions and redeployment in labor as part of the operating model, stating, “We’re going to experience significant reduction in headcount,” while also saying some reductions may be redeployed because “Customer service does not have enough to my liking.”

  • Lemonis framed macro sensitivity but said the operating plan assumes limited housing help, stating the model “assumes no inflection in the housing market,” and referenced rates with “the 10-year treasury is north of 4%” and “mortgage rates are north of 6%.”

Final Takeaway

Management presented Q1 as an inflection point marked by year-over-year revenue growth alongside a low operating cost structure, while reiterating a strategy of integrating acquired “capabilities” into a three-pillar “Everything Home” ecosystem. Leadership highlighted near-term integration costs (including a stated Q2 one-time expense load) but paired that with a quantified cost-out plan over the next nine months and specific operating targets for The Container Store footprint and longer-term EBITDA margin goals.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
